summary: Gruber reports on the results of his experiment with a full-content RSS feed supported by weekly sponsorships. Both concerns — sponsor interest and web traffic deflation — proved unfounded, so he's making the format permanent. He also added a second articles-only feed for readers who don't want frequent Linked List items. The post details his experimentation with how Linked List items link in the feed, ultimately keeping the original format where titles point away from Daring Fireball to the linked content, which he considers the better design.
key_points: The full-content sponsored RSS feed experiment succeeded: sponsorships sold briskly and web traffic remained near record highs despite giving everything away in the feed.
Gruber added a second articles-only feed to satisfy readers who wanted less frequent updates without Linked List items.
He tested changing Linked List feed links to point back to Daring Fireball permalinks, but reversed course after receiving far more complaints about the change than the original format.
Gruber argues the Linked List's purpose is to send readers elsewhere, and the feed format should reflect that design intent rather than follow conventions from other sites.
The permalink marker for Linked List items in the feed evolved from '#' to '★' to match the web site convention.
money_quotes: So, let's remove the "experimental" label and make it permanent.
My goal is to maintain a balance between simplicity and reader satisfaction.
Just one feed is as simple as it gets, but I agree there's a need for a less-frenetic articles-only feed.
Within one day I'd received far more complaints about the change than I had gotten about the original format in the course of a week.
The point of the Linked List is to send you away from Daring Fireball to read or look at something elsewhere, and the feed format should reflect that.
I suspect the reason some readers asked for the other style is that it's what they're accustomed to from other feeds. I'm convinced this is a better design, however.
key_insight: A full-content RSS feed supported by sponsorships can sustain a publishing business without cannibalizing web traffic, and feed design should reflect the editorial intent of the content rather than follow generic conventions.
